- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	**UPDATE: This release has been deleted and postponed until we figure out an
2	upgrade path for the new header**
3	
4	Strongbox v0.3.1 is now released and is available for download from:
5	https://github.com/uw-labs/strongbox/releases/tag/v0.3.1
6	
7	## changes
8	
9	This release comes with a fairly big change of having a different format of
10	ciphertext header.
11	
12	Previous versions all decorated files with a static string of:
13	
14	```
15	# STRONGBOX ENCRYPTED RESOURCE ; See https://github.com/uw-labs/strongbox
16	```
17	
18	The new version now decorates with the following:
19	
20	```
21	# STRONGBOX ENCRYPTED RESOURCE ; See https://github.com/uw-labs/strongbox
22	# key-id: 2xnNh194YZw/80PXOeY1HDGEhxFKt+QmX0jhDWOLhAQ=
23	```
24	
25	This change was mostly driven by two issues.
26	
27	1. If you have a file managed by strongbox that alphanumerically precedes
28	  `.strongbox-keyid`, strongbox will fail to decrypt it on the first pass,
29	  since the `.strongbox-keyid` will be absent on disk when your file is being
30	  checked out.
31	
32	With the addition of key-id in the header, we can use that to decrypt files,
33	instead of the key-id file on disk.
34	
35	2. Presented with a strongbox file, its not trivial to know which key you need
36	  to be able to decrypt it.
37	
38	This can happen if a team is using multiple keys to segregate security
39	concerns. Having key-id present in the header, you now can easily identify the
40	key you are after.
41	
42	It does however mean that modifying the secret file or `touch`ing it, can
43	create a git diff, even if there are no changes to the plain-text.
44	
45	You can verify the changes to the header metadata using: `git diff-index HEAD -p`
46	
47	## compatibility
48	
49	0.1.0 - is not capable of decrypting multi-line headers, it will fail to
50	decrypt files encrypted with 0.3.1
51	
52	0.2.0 - is capable of decrypting multi-line headers (as well as single-line),
53	but by default encrypts files with the old, single-line header. This means if
54	you modify a secret that was previously encrypted with 0.3.1, *you will strip
55	the prefix and revert it back to a single line*. In git it will show up as
56	"changed file", but `git diff` will show no changes. `git diff-index HEAD -p`
57	will show you the header changes.
58	
59	0.3.1 - will decrypt both single and multi-line headers, but by default
60	encrypts using the multi-line format.
61

1	# Strongbox support for age encryption
2	
3	age (https://github.com/FiloSottile/age) is a fantastic encryption tool and a
4	library that very neatly fits the Strongbox flow.
5	
6	At the time of Strongbox's creation this was not available and so we used a SIV
7	library: https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/jacobsa/crypto/siv .
8	
9	This worked well enough for years but now that age is available there is a list
10	of reasons why we would want to support it:
11	
12	- very strong encryption, Edward's curves, ChaCha20 and Bech32 encoding *Chef's kiss*
13	- support for armor, means we don't need to implement encoding and compression
14	- can use `age` tool outside of Strongbox flow to operate on secrets directly
15	- age supports multiple recipients, multiple keys can decrypt the same secret
16	
17	# How to use it
18	
19	The new age support has been implemented to be backward compatible with our
20	existing workflow.
21	
22	**You can continue using Strongbox v2 without any changes with existing keys.**
23	
24	There is a new flag to generate an age (v2) key: `-gen-identity`, usage section
25	of the README has been updated with instructions for the new keys:
26	https://github.com/uw-labs/strongbox?tab=readme-ov-file#usage
27	
28	Re-encrypting secrets with the new key should be as simple as replacing the
29	`.strongbox-keyid` with `.strongbox_recipient` that contains the new key(s).
30

1	# Tech Stack
2	
3	Documentation for the various bits of technology that we use at UW. Everything
4	in here should be synced to the ["Tech Stack" topic in
5	Slab](https://wiki.uw.systems/topics/tech-stack-dv4mkxkx).
6	
7	Documentation lives (or, more commonly, dies) by them being actively interacted
8	with and kept up-to-date. If you notice something missing or incorrect in these
9	docs please don't hesitate to raise an issue or a pull request.
10	
11	## Structure
12	
13	We are experimenting with having a fixed structure across each technology here,
14	currently it is:
15	
16	  - `getting_started.md`: A quick introduction to the technology, where and what
17	    we use it for at UW, and any helpful links
18	  - `best_practices.md`: Best practices for using this piece of technology at UW
19	  - `how_to.md`: How to get something done with the technology, if not covered
20	    by the official docs
21	  - `learnings.md`: Lessons we've learned from using and experimenting with this
22	    technology at UW. Can be helpful in giving some historical perspective to
23	    decisions.
24	  - `explanation.md`: A deeper dive into any technical details (generally
25	    covered by official docs)
26	
27	Any single file above can be replaced by a directory, e.g. instead of a single
28	`how_to.md` there could be `how_to/README.md` for all the miscellaneous/small
29	bits, and `how_to/some_complicated_process.md` to cover some specific process.
30	
31	It is currently a work-in-progress to translate existing docs over to this
32	format, but for now you can see it in:
33	
34	  - [`grpc`](./grpc)
35	  - [`docker`](./docker)
36	  - [`telemetry`](./telemetry)
37	  - [`kustomize`](./kustomize)
38	
39	## Guidelines
40	
41	## Code examples should be as close to their source as possible
42	
43	This is due to the likelihood of such docs to go out-of-sync (e.g. documenting
44	usage of a function whose signature changes):
45	
46	  - A code example in these docs for some internal library is likely to go out
47	    of sync over time.
48	  - A code example in the README of some internal library is less likely to go
49	    out of sync over time.
50	  - A code example that is also run as a test in an internal library is
51	    guaranteed to stay in sync, e.g. [testable Go
52	    examples](https://go.dev/blog/examples)
53

1	# Using [kube-applier](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier)
2	
3	## Summary
4	
5	This document refers to kube-applier v3 as implemented in our
6	[fork](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/) of the original
7	project.
8	
9	kube-applier enables continuous deployment of Kubernetes resources by applying
10	declarative configuration files from a Git repository to a kubernetes cluster.
11	
12	Each cluster has its own instance of kube-applier running that watches the
13	`master` branch of the
14	[`kubernetes-manifests`](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kubernetes-manifests)
15	repository and runs `kubectl` to keep the cluster state in sync with the state
16	of the repository.
17	
18	## Usage
19	
20	You need the following to use KA:
21	
22	- Waybill
23	- Delegate ServiceAccount + RBAC
24	
25	Optionally you can also have:
26	
27	- Strongbox integration
28	- Kustomize using multiple private remote bases
29	
30	### Waybill
31	
32	Configuration for each namespace is done through the
33	[`Waybill`](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/#waybill-crd)
34	resource. Add the definition for your namespace:
35	
36	```
37	apiVersion: kube-applier.io/v1alpha1
38	kind: Waybill
39	metadata:
40	  name: main
41	```
42	
43	### Delegate ServiceAccount + RBAC
44	
45	In order to apply files in a namespace, kube-applier requires a [delegate
46	ServiceAccount](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/#delegate-serviceaccount).
47	
48	If you are using `kustomize` for your namespace, you can pull in the "client"
49	base from the kube-applier repository:
50	
51	```
52	bases:
53	  - github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier//manifests/base/client?ref=v3.3.7
54	```
55	
56	Make sure you're using the latest version, found in the [kube-applier
57	releases](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/releases).
58	
59	If you are **not** using `kustomize`, you can copy the [relevant
60	file](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/blob/master/manifests/base/client/service-account.yaml)
61	from from the base above.
62	
63	### Strongbox
64	
65	If you have secrets encrypted with
66	[`strongbox`](https://github.com/uw-labs/strongbox) in your namespace,
67	kube-applier will need the key to decrypt files before applying. To do this we
68	create a Kubernetes Secret that contains a Strongbox keyring.
69	
70	Kustomize
71	
72	```
73	secretGenerator:
74	  - name: kube-applier-strongbox-keyring
75	    type: Opaque
76	    files:
77	      - .strongbox_keyring=secrets/strongbox-keyring
78	    options:
79	      disableNameSuffixHash: true
80	```
81	
82	Plain manifest
83	
84	```
85	kind: Secret
86	apiVersion: v1
87	metadata:
88	  name: kube-applier-strongbox-keyring
89	stringData:
90	  .strongbox_keyring: |-
91	      keyentries:
92	      - description: keyname
93	        key-id: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA=
94	        key: AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA=
95	```
96	
97	Make sure that the keyring Secret itself **is encrypted** in git.
98	
99	Reference the Secret in your Waybill:
100	
101	```
102	apiVersion: kube-applier.io/v1alpha1
103	kind: Waybill
104	metadata:
105	  name: main
106	spec:
107	  strongboxKeyringSecretRef:
108	    name: kube-applier-strongbox-keyring
109	```
110	
111	See the kube-applier documentation
112	[section](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/#integration-with-strongbox)
113	for more information, such as how to setup shared strongbox keyrings.
114	
115	### Using private repositories for kustomize bases
116	
117	Kustomize
118	
119	```
120	secretGenerator:
121	  - name: kube-applier-git-ssh
122	    type: Opaque
123	    files:
124	      - key_a=secrets/ssh-keys/a
125	      - key_b=secrets/ssh-keys/b
126	    options:
127	      disableNameSuffixHash: true
128	```
129	
130	Plain manifest
131	
132	```
133	kind: Secret
134	apiVersion: v1
135	metadata:
136	  name: kube-applier-git-ssh
137	stringData:
138	  key_a: |-
139	    -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
140	    AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==
141	    -----END OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
142	  key_b: |-
143	    -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
144	    AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==
145	    -----END OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----
146	  known_hosts: |-
147	    github.com ssh-rsa AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA==
148	```
149	
150	
151	As shown in the example, you can define multiple SSH keys. For each private
152	repository to be used, you will need SSH key with access to it. For example,
153	you can use a single key that can access all these repositories or you can use
154	one deploy key for each repository. **The names of the SSH keys in the `Secret`
155	must be prefixed with `"key_"`.**
156	
157	In order to use this SSH key for fetching `kustomize` bases, the bases should
158	be defined with the `ssh://` scheme in `kustomization.yaml` and have a comment
159	above referencing the SSH key: `# kube-applier: key_a`. For example:
160	
161	```
162	bases:
163	  # https scheme (default if omitted), any SSH keys defined are ignored
164	  - github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier//testdata/bases/simple-deployment?ref=master
165	  # ssh scheme requires a valid SSH key to be defined
166	  # kube-applier: key_a
167	  - ssh://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier//testdata/bases/simple-deployment?ref=master
168	```
169	
170	The `known_hosts` item is optional and if ommitted, `git` will use `ssh` with
171	`StrictHostKeyChecking` disabled.
172	
173	You can share this `Secret` between namespaces in the same way that it is done
174	for the strongbox keyring Secret. Please refer to the `kube-applier`
175	documentation for details.
176	
177	This `Secret` should be referenced in the `Waybill` spec:
178	```
179	apiVersion: kube-applier.io/v1alpha1
180	kind: Waybill
181	metadata:
182	  name: main
183	spec:
184	  gitSSHSecretRef:
185	    name: kube-applier-git-ssh
186	```
187	
188	
189	### Clean up from v2
190	
191	If you are migrating from the older version of kube-applier, you only need to
192	remove the relevant annotations from your namespace definition.
193	
194	
195	### Final steps
196	
197	Before kube-applier can manage your namespace, you need to apply all these new
198	resources manually. This is only required once; kube-applier will ignore
199	namespaces that do not contain a Waybill resource and it cannot apply anything
200	without access to a delegate ServiceAccount token (and a strongbox keyring, if
201	strongbox is used).
202	
203	## Monitoring
204	
205	You can get information about the status of a namespace either by visiting the
206	status page:
207	
208	- https://kube-applier-system.dev.aws.uw.systems/
209	- https://kube-applier-system.dev.gcp.uw.systems/
210	- https://kube-applier-system.dev.merit.uw.systems/
211	- https://kube-applier-system.prod.aws.uw.systems/
212	- https://kube-applier-system.prod.gcp.uw.systems/
213	- https://kube-applier-system.prod.merit.uw.systems/
214	
215	or on the command line, eg.:
216	
217	```
218	kubectl --context dev-merit -n my-namespace describe waybill
219	```
220	
221	If you don't see new runs being recorded for your namespace, it is worth
222	checking events in your namespace. A number of issues that can occur outside of
223	`kubectl` will be recorded as events:
224	
225	```
226	kubectl --context dev-merit -n my-namespace get events --field-selector=involvedObject.kind=Waybill
227	```
228	
229	kube-applier exports a number of [prometheus
230	metrics](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kube-applier/#metrics). We also
231	provide a [grafana
232	dashboard](https://grafana.dev.merit.uw.systems/d/6WCRTQAMz/kube-applier-v3)
233	for monitoring the kube-applier deployment and managed namespace.
234	
235	If you want to setup alerts for your namespace, you can refer to the [system
236	alerts](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/kubernetes-manifests/blob/master/dev-merit/sys-prom/resources/prometheus-alerts.yaml#L397-L452).
237	
238	## Additional kube-applier deployments
239	
240	If you require running your own instance of kube-applier v3, please get in
241	touch. This is not supported properly at the moment but it is something we can
242	work on if a use case demands for it.
243	
244	kube-applier v3 has been designed in such a way that there is proper
245	segregation between managed namespaces, their resources and secrets. For that
246	reason it has been developed with a single instance per cluster in mind and
247	multiple instances could potentially conflict with each other.
248

1	Software development practices at UW
2	====================================
3	
4	Introduction
5	------------
6	
7	At UW in IT, we value the ability for teams to have maximum flexibility, with room to experiment, innovate,
8	learn and improve both in terms of processes and in terms of technology choices.
9	
10	In practice, this means that we:
11	
12	* Avoid standardisation for the sake of having standards.
13	* Encourage de-coupling from any particular vendor or product, in order to keep the cost of experimentation low.
14	* Avoid monocultures of approach or technology.
15	
16	In a few limited areas, where we do see it necessary to standardise, we try to chose approaches or
17	technologies that impose very little constraint on teams. (Examples here are the adoption of docker and kubernetes).
18	
19	Also, we have seen that over time, certain patterns, approaches, and technologies emerge as de-facto
20	standards, and in some of these cases, we benefit from the universality of approach. This document lists some of
21	those standard practices and some of the reasons behind adopting them.
22	
23	This is not a rule-book, but a list of approaches that are becoming more widely used, and so in also using these
24	(unless you have a good reason not to) you can make your work more familiar and more approachable to others,
25	which especially helps when the need arises to interact or integrate across more than one team.
26	
27	The basic high-level stuff
28	--------------------------
29	
30	We try to run everything we can from docker images in our k8s cluster.
31	
32	We prefer the flexibility and portability that comes with deploying as much as we sensibly can inside of k8s,
33	to the often simpler approach of relying on cloud vendor specific managed services. This means accepting a small
34	up-front and on-going overhead, in preference to a longer term, harder to measure exit cost in leaving a provider.
35	Remaining portable has greater value now that we have dev and prod environments in GCP as well as AWS.
36	
37	We believe in micro-service architectures. It's important to understand the difference between a true microservices
38	architecture and a distributed monolith.
39	
40	Solution & data architecture
41	----------------------------
42	
43	We prefer event driven, ideally event sourced architectural approach. This keeps data modelling flexible, is
44	well suited to properly designed micro-service architectures, and is particularly well suited to migration efforts
45	such as the "getting out of Bill" work we are currently in the middle of.
46	
47	We believe in domain driven design principles, and believe high level systems architecture should reflect the
48	structure of the business. We believe in individual system components having a well defined and understood
49	responsibility.
50	
51	We do avoid sharing state between components through shared database or filesystem access.
52	Instead we prefer event passing.
53	
54	Event modelling
55	---------------
56	
57	We prefer fine grained events to coarse grained events. In other words, we represent a single change in a
58	single event where possible, and we are comfortable with having multiple events needed by a consumer to build
59	a complete or meaningful data set.  We also recognise that sometimes, multiple things can change in the same event.
60	
61	We strongly prefer informative, not imperative events. For example, `OrderSubmitted` is an informative description of something that happened, whereas `CreateOrder` is an imperative to take an action. This is problematic because it implies that the event producer has to have an understanding of the possible outcomes. By writing consumers that are reactive to informative events, we are able to build flexible systems that can absorb complexity and changing requirements.
62	
63	We design events to represent a business-meaningful change that happened in the real world. Sometimes, we supplement
64	these events with further (calculated) events that model important business concepts for us, where such calculation
65	is non-trivial and is a publicly (outside UW staff) concept (an example is "partner status").
66	
67	Technical formats for events
68	----------------------------
69	
70	### Protocol buffers
71	
72	We prefer google protocol buffers for modelling our events, because they are readable and changeable by non-developers
73	and represent a compact, high performance format, with convenient code generation for developers.
74	
75	We follow the google style guide for protocol buffers.
76	
77	We typically put our events in envelopes and use `Any` to carry the payload. This gives good flexibility
78	(compared to `OneOf`) at the cost of a slightly larger payload.  Teams often have their own envelopes for
79	internal use, but for cross team or cross-domain usage, we try to stick to a single shared envelope format found
80	at https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/event-envelope-proto
81	
82	We model enums in protobuf where the `0` value represents `none` or `default` or `unknown` etc, rather than a real value.
83	
84	We prefix enums in protobuf with the type name due to how protobuf namespaces work. (If this creates stuttering names
85	after generation, look at alternative generator options)
86	
87	Many teams prefer keeping their proto code away from generated code, for a few reasons. One reason is that different
88	applications using the protos may have reasons to use different proto generators, or different versions or
89	configurations of proto generators, and this can result in incompatible code, often that compiles but fails in
90	interesting ways at runtime. Another reason is that we don't all use Go. We also use protobuf from js and starting
91	to from Rust. We don't want all of this in a single repo.
92	
93	We find that generating code in the application that uses it avoids various problems associated with shared code.
94	
95	### JSON
96	
97	Where we can't use protocol buffers (for example 3rd party integration) we try to use JSON if possible.
98	
99	We also use JSON internally in some cases. (TODO: where and why?)
100

37	
38	## Argo CD @ UW 
39	
40	Argo CD controllers are deployed in `sys-argocd` namespace in all UW clusters. Its running in `cluster-scope` mode.
41	Each Argo CD instance manages its own cluster and cross cluster deployments are not supported.
42	We use `Applications in any namespace` feature to define and manage Application in destination namespace instead of `sys-argocd`.
43	This means teams can deploy and maintain own Applications.
44	
45	Regarding `ApplicationSet`, its main purpose is to enable flexibility in managing Application across multiple cluster.
46	Since this is not supported, applicationSet controllers are **not** deployed in our Argo CD deployments.
47	
48	We run [argocd-voodoobox-plugin](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/argocd-voodoobox-plugin)
49	as "Sidecar plugin" which is responsible for decryption and running
50	`kustomize`.
51	
52	* [dev-aws](https://argocd-system.dev.aws.uw.systems/)
53	* [dev-gcp](https://argocd-system.dev.gcp.uw.systems/)
54	* [dev-merit](https://argocd-system.dev.merit.uw.systems/)
55	* [prod-aws](https://argocd-system.prod.aws.uw.systems/)
56	* [prod-gcp](https://argocd-system.prod.gcp.uw.systems/)
57	* [prod-merit](https://argocd-system.prod.merit.uw.systems/)
58	
59	There are 2 main resources in Argo CD: `project` and `application`.
60	
61	### Projects
62	Projects provide a logical grouping of Applications. A Project represents a team in UW.
63	
64	Projects provide the following features:
65	* define list of git repositories applications are allowed as source
66	* define namespaces where applications can be found for this project and also destination where its allowed to deploy
67	* restrict applications access, normally Argo CD apps are only given namespace level access
68	* defining project roles to provide application RBAC
69	
70	There should be at least 1 project per Team. Projects are defined in `sys-argocd` namespace.
71	Teams should create and maintain its own projects/applications. 
72	Since Project is an cluster scoped resource, it needs to be approved and applied by infra/system team. Team can also set RBAC role for the project and applications in project resource. We use SSO via Okta, hence okta groups can be used in RBAC.
73	
74	Project example, please see [Project Specification](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/operator-manual/project-specification/) for all options
75	
76	```yaml
77	apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
78	kind: AppProject
79	metadata:
80	  name: <team>
81	  # Finalizer that ensures that project is not deleted until it is not referenced by any application
82	  finalizers:
83	    - resources-finalizer.argocd.argoproj.io
84	spec:
85	  description: project for team's namespaces
86	  sourceRepos:
87	    - git@github.com:utilitywarehouse/kubernetes-manifests.git
88	  sourceNamespaces:
89	    - <team_namespace>
90	  destinations:
91	    - namespace: <team_namespace>
92	      name: in-cluster
93	  orphanedResources:
94	    # https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/orphaned-resources/
95	    warn: true
96	    ignore:
97	      # vault-tls is automatically created for each NS
98	      - group: ""
99	        kind: ConfigMap
100	        name: vault-tls
101	      # kyverno policies automatically created by system
102	      - group: wgpolicyk8s.io
103	        kind: PolicyReport
104	  roles:
105	    # p, proj:<project>:<role-name>, <resource>, <action>, <project>/<namespace>/<application> <effect>
106	    # Resources: applications, repositories, clusters, logs, exec
107	    # Actions: get, create, update, delete, sync, override, action/<group/kind/action-name>
108	    - name: admin
109	      description: admin access to all applications under this project
110	      policies:
111	        - p, proj:okta:admin, applications, *, <team>/*, allow
112	        - p, proj:okta:admin, applications, delete, <team>/*, deny
113	      groups:
114	        - user@uw.co.uk
115	        - team_group
116	```
117	Its very easy to accidentally delete app with cascade delete via GUI hence we suggest deny all delete ops from GUI. [Deletion Using kubectl](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/app_deletion/#deletion-using-kubectl) 
118	
119	### Applications
120	Application is an Argo CD `CRD`. Its provides logical grouping of k8s resources.
121	An `application` can represent whole namespace (like waybill in KA). 
122	It is defined by 3 key pieces of information:
123	* `source` reference to the desired state in Git (repository, revision, path, environment). 
124	   argocd plugin related information is also defined here.
125	* `destination` reference to the target cluster and namespace. For the cluster one of server or name can be used, but not both (which will result in an error). name of the cluster is same `in-cluster` in all clusters
126	* `project` The project the application belongs to. team should reference their own projects.
127	  The application's namesapce should be listed in this projects `sourceNamespaces` & `destinations` fields.
128	
129	
130	Example, please see [Application Specification](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/application-specification/) for all options
131	```yaml
132	apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
133	kind: Application
134	metadata:
135	  name: <ns_name>
136	  labels:
137	    name: <ns_name>
138	  annotations:
139	    argocd.argoproj.io/manifest-generate-paths: .
140	spec:
141	  project: <team_project>
142	  source:
143	    repoURL: git@github.com:utilitywarehouse/kubernetes-manifests.git
144	    targetRevision: master
145	  destination:
146	    name: in-cluster
147	    namespace: <ns_name>
148	  syncPolicy:
149	    syncOptions:
150	      - ApplyOutOfSyncOnly=true
151	```
152	#### Sync Policy
153	Argo CD has the ability to automatically sync an application when it detects differences between the desired manifests in Git, and the live state in the cluster.
154	Following config will enable automatic sync with `prune`. if `prune` is enabled, it will delete resources when Argo CD detects the resource is no longer defined in Git.
155	```yaml
156	spec:
157	  syncPolicy:
158	    automated:
159	      prune: true
160	```
161	
162	If you want to manage enabling/disabling sync via GUI or kubectl then its best to not commit `automated: {}` in Git. 
163	
164	#### finalizer
165	Without the `resources-finalizer.argocd.argoproj.io` finalizer, deleting an application will not delete the resources it manages. To perform a cascading delete, you must add the finalizer.
166	To avoid accidental deletion of all namespace resources managed under application we suggest **avoid** adding finalizer. [See App Deletion](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/app_deletion/#about-the-deletion-finalizer). 
167	
168	## argocd-voodoobox-plugin
169	argocd doesn't support `strongbox` natively. To add this support we have created our own argocd plugin called [argocd-voodoobox-plugin](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/argocd-voodoobox-plugin).
170	
171	#### decryption
172	In terms of decryption this plugin behaves exactly as `Kube Applier`. 
173	By default it will try to read `argocd-voodoobox-strongbox-keyring` secret from its own namespace to get `.strongbox_keyring` data just like KA.
174	It also supports getting keyring data from other namespace. For that plugin env `STRONGBOX_SECRET_NAMESPACE` must be set as shown. 
175	
176	```yaml
177	# argocd application configuration in namespace ns-a reading strongbox keyring form ns-b
178	spec:
179	  source:
180	    repoURL: git@github.com/my-org/app.git
181	    targetRevision: HEAD
182	    plugin:
183	      env:
184	        - name: STRONGBOX_SECRET_NAMESPACE
185	          value: ns-b
186	```
187	
188	```yaml
189	# secret configuration in namespace ns-b allowing ns-a to read SB data
190	kind: Secret
191	apiVersion: v1
192	metadata:
193	  name: argocd-voodoobox-strongbox-keyring
194	  namespace: <target_namespace>
195	  annotations:
196	    argocd.voodoobox.plugin.io/allowed-namespaces: "ns-a"
197	stringData:
198	  .strongbox_keyring: |-
199	    keyentries:
200	    - description: mykey

1. Start the project by creating a **new folder** in the [terraform repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform) in the root for [dev-aws](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/tree/master/aws/dev) or [prod-aws](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/tree/master/aws/prod) . This will be a new Terraform module with its own state.
2. Define the S3 backend where the state of the terraform project is kept. [Example](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/backend.tf).
   **Make sure to change the key** if you copied it.
3. Create a symlink to the root aws **env.tf var file for [dev](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/**env.tf).
4. Import the [remote state from sys-outputs](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/remote-state.tf) to get access to other useful variables
5. The database instance is configured using a master password that is encrypted using strongbox.
   - add your team strongbox key to the project. [Example](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/.strongbox-keyid).
   - define the master password in a file called `terraform.tfvars`. The contents should be:
     `root_password = "your_chosen_password" `
   - mark this file as encrypted by strongbox. [Example](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/.gitattributes)
6. Define the RDS instance. [Example](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/main.tf)
   - adjust the [team name](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/209eeb5fb006fe584fe0e3526c8fd42be7d3e93e/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/main.tf#L4)
   - adjust the [name of the RDS instance](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/209eeb5fb006fe584fe0e3526c8fd42be7d3e93e/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/main.tf#L2)
   - declare the [root_password variable](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/209eeb5fb006fe584fe0e3526c8fd42be7d3e93e/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/main.tf#L8-L10) that will be fetched from terraform.tfvars encrypted file
   - we need a security group defined. You can copy as is from [here](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/209eeb5fb006fe584fe0e3526c8fd42be7d3e93e/aws/dev/sys-example-rds/main.tf#L12-L41)

1	# UW development and deployment process : security processes and controls
2	
3	## devsecops
4	
5	We encourage and promote a devsecops approach within our teams (see https://www.devsecops.org).
6	
7	This means we combine a culture of empowering developers to take personal responsibility for
8	security, with a "trust, verify & monitor" mindset.
9	
10	## development practices
11	
12	Teams routinely review each others code, either during development (pair programming) or after (PR review)
13	
14	All code, however developed has to be merged into the `master` in our revision control system before
15	it is built into a form deployable to production. In addition, a further change in our revision
16	control system is made before the application is actually deployed to our production system.
17	Both of these changes require review by least one other team member before progressing, and there
18	are typically technical controls in place to ensure that this happens.  All of these changes are also
19	available for review beyond the team, either by other teams, or by senior IT team members, and can be
20	inspected or searched at any future date.
21	
22	## auditing
23	
24	Because we use github for our version control, all changes made by anyone in our organisation are
25	logged and can be inspected. Where deemed appropriate, we can alert on any unusual or unexpected
26	behaviour based on this audit log.
27	
28	## infrastructure as code
29	
30	Much like our code changes, all changes to underlying infrastructure (virtual machines, storage, networking
31	setup) are managed via our revision control system and so are covered by exactly the same review and
32	auditing policies and controls.
33	
34	## immutable deployments
35	
36	Our deployed software is based on docker images which are immutable images that cannot be tampered with
37	undetectably. We have a policy of not manually modifying running containers in any way, and each time an
38	application is restarted, any changes which may have been made are automatically rolled back to the more
39	recently deployed version in our revision control system.
40	
41	
42	## resilient deployments
43	Below is a list of a few very simple manifest tweaks engineers can use in their Kubernetes deployments to ensure a more reliable and resilient operation.
44	
45	### Nodes can die, and will die
46	Pods are scheduled on nodes. Nodes within our infrastructure can disappear, and will disappear, for a plethora of reasons (failing node, platform upgrade etc). This is considered normal operation.
47	
48	### Replica count > 1
49	`replicas` specifies how many pods should be running for a given deployment. For obvious reasons we want this to be more than 1 whenever possible. Not always possible but we strive for this. 3 pods usually do the trick as it allows us to set sensible PDBs explained below.
50	
51	### Anti-affinity
52	We set this to make sure that all of our service's pods do not end up on the same node. Imagine spinning up 3 pods for a service thinking it's resilient and all of them getting scheduled on the same node. That node goes kaboom, so does the service.
53	
54	```yaml
55	spec:
56	      affinity:
57	        podAntiAffinity:
58	          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
59	            - podAffinityTerm:
60	                labelSelector:
61	                  matchExpressions:
62	                    - key: app
63	                      operator: In
64	                      values:
65	                        - my-service
66	                topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
67	              weight: 100
68	```
69	
70	### Pod Disruption Budget
71	Determines how many pods need to be up while rescheduling pods to other nodes. When the scheduler starts evicting pods from a node it will check the PDB and ensure the number of specified pods will always remain alive. Without PDB the scheduler could evict all pods of a service during rescheduling causing temporary service outage. **Services with 1 pod cannot have PDB 1 as this will stop node rolls.**
72	
73	```yaml
74	apiVersion: policy/v1beta1
75	kind: PodDisruptionBudget
76	metadata:
77	  name: my-service
78	spec:
79	  minAvailable: 2
80	  selector:
81	    matchLabels:
82	      app: my-service
83	
84	````
85	
86	### Image Pull Policy
87	Specifies whether our pod should always pull the docker image from the registry even if it’s already pulled it in the past. Useful for development environments where we use the latest/local tags and overwrite them endlessly but not so much for production where we use unique git hashes. Imagine a pod getting evicted due to a node roll and not being able to contact the docker registry (has happened) while rescheduling the pod. Because of this, the image pull policy should probably be set to `IfNotPresent` or left absent (it's set to this value by default).
88

content.content:
1	# Learnings
2	
3	Learnings from previous thoughts/experiments with Docker at UW
4	
5	## Docker registry
6	
7	The Docker registry at <https://registry.uw.systems> is based on the official
8	[registry image](https://hub.docker.com/_/registry/) and uses an S3 backend (see
9	[here](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/system-manifests/tree/4d5aafe087ba489d3fe30035cfd07cf6fe4d17f8/docker-registry)
10	for full details)
11	
12	Some consequences of this:
13	
14	  - Since data transfers into S3 is free, the cost of pushing an image is cheap
15	  - Since data storage in S3 is fairly cheap, storing images is fairly cheap
16	  - Since data transfers out of S3 is treated as egress, pulling images can be
17	    relatively expensive
18	
19	## Go Monorepo approaches
20	
21	### Build everything all the time
22	
23	This is the approach used by e.g.:
24	
25	  - [Dev Enablement mono
26	    repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/dev-enablement-mono/blob/2a94f748040e96a2deed76313bd65bc9bffdd20b/.github/workflows/docker.yaml#L6):
27	    using a manually maintained list of services
28	  - [IAM mono
29	    repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/iam/blob/73aaad54f59a3b1af609f46637547a155ed7fca8/.github/workflows/deploy.yaml#L15):
30	    which automatically generates the list of services
31	
32	The benefits are:
33	
34	  - Simplicity, just point Docker at a some subdirectory and let it build
35	  - Assuming images are tagged with a Git SHA, the current (or any previous) tip
36	    of the main branch are known to correspond to deployable images (so it's
37	    trivial to e.g. figure out where things can be rolled-back to)
38	
39	The downsides are:
40	
41	  - Cost of GHA actions. Since this approach adds a new job for each service,
42	    and since each job is billed at least 1 minute in GHA time (even if it hits
43	    all caches and only takes a couple of seconds) this can quickly add up to a
44	    significant amount when as the number of applications grows
45	  - Cost of registry traffic, since we push images on every single CI run
46	
47	### Determine which services are impacted by a change and build only those
48	
49	This is the approach used by e.g.:
50	
51	  - [CBC mono
52	    repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/cbc-mono/blob/8a7bc34ae89caaac2bf05fb09988faac0b4a1eb1/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L42)
53	  - [Partner mono
54	    repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/partner-mono/blob/a7d8dc3d0a09cb696a0d193695174f3be5e8c95a/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L41)
55	  - [Finance mono
56	    repo](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/finance-mono/blob/b879791d42d477e52710624764eb278d8158f8ae/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L165)
57	
58	The benefits are:
59	
60	  - Fewer builds since we build and release only what needs to be changed
61	
62	The downside are:
63	
64	  - Complexity in having to maintain a program to determine what's changed
65	  - It requires language specific approaches, e.g. if tomorrow we said we want
66	    to start mono repos for TypeScript backends we couldn't just copy over our
67	    solution for Go mono repos
68	  - Assuming images are tag with Git SHAs, it's not clear if an image exists for
69	    a given service on any commit that was the tip of the main branch
70	
71	## Docker with GitHub actions
72	
73	### Cache mounts
74	
75	[Cache
76	mounts](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/#run---mounttypecache)
77	are useful for being able to re-use e.g. Go mod caches and build caches between
78	Docker builds. Unfortunately, these aren't very useful in CI since there's no
79	way to preserve the cache between CI invocations (see [this
80	issue](https://github.com/moby/buildkit/issues/1512)).
81	
82	There are some [official
83	docs](https://docs.docker.com/build/ci/github-actions/cache/) for workarounds of
84	these issues.
85	
86	## Docker and Go Dependencies
87	
88	It is very useful to populate Go's module cache using just `go.mod` and `go.sum`
89	in a separate layer before copying up the rest of the source, like:
90	
91	``` dockerfile
92	FROM golang:1.20-alpine AS build
93	
94	WORKDIR /build
95	
96	# Layer cache depends only on go.{mod,sum}
97	# the downloads will be cached until something is changed there
98	COPY go.mod .
99	COPY go.sum .
100	RUN go mod download
101	
102	# Layer cache depends on any changes in '.', so application (and dependencies)
103	# will be recompiled any time there's a change there
104	COPY . .
105	RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o /app ./path/to/app
106	```
107	
108	It would be nice to be able to also populate Go's build cache from just `go.mod`
109	and `go.sum`, i.e. compile all dependencies so that the `go build` steps only
110	needs to require our application and link everything together. However, it
111	appears there's currently no language support for such a setup, per these
112	issues:
113	
114	  - <https://github.com/golang/go/issues/45474>
115	  - <https://github.com/golang/go/issues/27719>
116	
117	## Interacting with the Docker API
118	
119	There are likely more nicely packaged ways to interact with this, but poking
120	around manually can be a useful learning experience (and useful when you have
121	nothing else).
122	
123	### Authentication
124	
125	The first thing is to authenticate, since trying to access the API will return
126	an authentication error:
127	
128	``` console
129	$ curl https://registry.uw.systems/v2/
130	{
131	  "errors": [
132	    {
133	      "code": "UNAUTHORIZED",
134	      "message": "authentication required",
135	      "detail": null
136	    }
137	  ]
138	}
139	```
140	
141	The specification for that is defined
142	[here](https://distribution.github.io/distribution/spec/auth/token/) that
143	suggests we should look at the `WWW-Authenticate` header
144	
145	``` console
146	$ curl --write-out '%{header_json}\n' https://registry.uw.systems/v2/ 
147	{
148	  "errors": [
149	    {
150	      "code": "UNAUTHORIZED",
151	      "message": "authentication required",
152	      "detail": null
153	    }
154	  ]
155	}
156	{
157	  "content-type": [
158	    "application/json; charset=utf-8"
159	  ],
160	  "date": [
161	    "Thu, 26 Oct 2023 10:03:04 GMT"
162	  ],
163	  "docker-distribution-api-version": [
164	    "registry/2.0"
165	  ],
166	  "www-authenticate": [
167	    "Bearer realm=\"https://registry.uw.systems/auth\",service=\"registry\""
168	  ],
169	  "content-length": [
170	    "87"
171	  ]
172	}
173	```
174	
175	So let's try and request a token with the realm given from that header (See
176	[docs](https://distribution.github.io/distribution/spec/auth/token/#requesting-a-token).
177	You likely have some basic credentials stored in your Docker config that can be
178	used here:
179	
180	``` console
181	$ jq --raw-output '.auths."registry.uw.systems".auth' < ~/.docker/config.json
182	```
183	
184	Then requesting a token:
185	
186	``` console
187	$ curl \
188	    --silent \
189	    --header "authorization: Basic $BASIC_TOKEN" \
190	    'https://registry.uw.systems/auth?service=registry' \
191	    | jq --raw-output '.access_token'
192	```
193	
194	And using that to make a request:
195	
196	``` console
197	$ curl \
198	    --header "authorization: Bearer $TOKEN_FROM_AUTH" \
199	    https://registry.uw.systems/v2/
200	{}
201	```
202	
203	### Example: listing repositories
204	
205	See [API spec](https://distribution.github.io/distribution/spec/api/) using our
206	token from above:
207	
208	``` console
209	$ curl \
210	    --header "authorization: Bearer $TOKEN_FROM_AUTH" \
211	    https://registry.uw.systems/v2/_catalog
212	{
213	  "errors": [
214	    {
215	      "code": "UNAUTHORIZED",
216	      "message": "authentication required",
217	      "detail": [
218	        {
219	          "Type": "registry",
220	          "Class": "",
221	          "Name": "catalog",
222	          "Action": "*"
223	        }
224	      ]
225	    }
226	  ]
227	}
228	```
229	
230	Let's inspect the authentication header again:
231	
232	``` console
233	$ curl \
234	    --write-out '%{header_json}\n' \
235	    --silent \
236	    --output /dev/null \
237	    --header "authorization: Bearer $TOKEN_FROM_AUTH" \
238	    https://registry.uw.systems/v2/_catalog \
239	    | jq '."www-authenticate"'
240	[
241	  "Bearer realm=\"https://registry.uw.systems/auth\",service=\"registry\",scope=\"registry:catalog:*\",error=\"insufficient_scope\""
242	]
243	```
244	
245	So we should fetch a new token with `service=registry&scope=registry:catalog:*`,
246	once we have that we can query:
247	
248	``` console
249	$ curl \
250	    --silent \
251	    --header "authorization: Bearer $NEW_TOKEN" \
252	    https://registry.uw.systems/v2/_catalog \
253	    | jq --raw-output
254	{
255	  "repositories": [
256	    "account-platform/account-api",
257	    "account-platform/account-api-account-projector",
258	    "account-platform/account-api-atomic-projector",
259	    "account-platform/account-api-create-account-projector",

1 # Vault: Github credentials 2 3 We have added [`vault-plugin-secrets-github`](https://github.com/martinbaillie/vault-plugin-secrets-github?tab=readme-ov-file#vault-plugin-secrets-github) 4 plugin to create ephemeral, finely-scoped `Github` access tokens using Vault. 5 6 ## Why? 7 8 For performing automation against GitHub APIs, or in CI/CD, we normally use OAuth Tokens (PAT). 9 These tokens are tied to a user account, have very broadly-scoped permissions and do not expire. 10 There are number of such tokens ( eg `DEPLOY_GITHUB_TOKEN` ) shared at org level for all repositories. 11 12 GitHub Apps offer a better approach to this automation problem: 13 - They do not consume a seat (license), nor need credential management. 14 - They have much finer-grained permissions available to the access tokens. 15 - The tokens they issue expire after one hour. 16 17 However, GitHub Apps require the management of at least one private key which is 18 needed to mint the JWTs used for the App installation authentication token request flow. 19 If we share private key of the app with all repos, then it's similar to sharing Token. 20 Creating applications per repository is not practical as well. 21 This plugin addresses these problems and allows us to share one application between 22 all repositories, but still maintaining finely scoped access. 23 24 ## How it works? 25 26 ### Authentication 27 Workflow authenticates to Vault using OIDC token generated by Github for the workflow at runtime. 28 Vault is configured to trust GitHub's OIDC as a federated identity, it will match 29 the `claims` in workflow's jwt against the Vault jwt's roles 30 [bound_claims](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/system-terraform-modules/blob/main/vault_github_repo_permission_set/main.tf#L29-L46). 31 32 The workflow can log in to its repo specific role, or one of the shared roles. 33 34 ### Token Request 35 The Vault plugin acts as an intermediary for a GitHub App. The scope of the token 36 will be constrained by pre-configured `permission set`. 37 Once workflow is authenticated, it can request Github tokens based on permission set 38 from the plugin. The plugin, in turn, authenticates with the GitHub App and requests a token on 39 behalf of the workflow. 40 41 We manage permission sets and roles via terraform module [sys-vault-repo-permission-sets](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/tree/master/github/sys-vault-repo-permission-sets). 42 43 This flow is better explained in the sequence diagram below: 44 45 ```mermaid 46 sequenceDiagram 47 participant W as Workflow 48 participant V as Vault 49 participant G as GitHub 50 51 V->>G: fetch Github jwks 52 G-->>V: 53 54 W->>G: Request OIDC Token 55 G-->>W: jwt 56 57 Note over W: Auth (role: github_repo_org_reponame) 58 W->>V: POST /v1/auth/github-jwt/login 59 activate V 60 V->>V: Validate JWT 61 V->>V: Match JWT claims against the role’s bound_claims 62 V-->>W: vault access_token 63 deactivate V 64 65 Note over W: Request a GitHub token 66 W->>V: POST /v1/github/token/repo_org_reponame 67 activate V 68 Note over V: Get token constrained by that pre-configured permission set 69 V->>G: POST api.github.com/.../access_tokens 70 G-->>V: token: [ "v1.123456789..." exp: "1h" ] 71 V-->>W: [ token: "v1.123456789..." exp: "1h" ] 72 deactivate V 73 74 Note over W: Use access token on GitHub 75 W->>G: $ git clone https://x-access-token:v1.123456789...@github.com/org/repo.git 76 W->>G: $ curl -H "Authorization: Bearer v1.123456789..." https://api.github.com/resource 77 ``` 78 79 ## hashicorp/vault-action 80 81 The `hashicorp/vault-action` action provides a way for GitHub Actions workflow 82 to authenticate with `Vault` and retrieve secrets. 83 The action uses the `role` to `log in` and get a vault token, 84 and then uses that token to read the secret from a specific Path. 85 86 A Vault `role` is a set of policies that define which `secrets` actions can access. 87 88 A Vault `secret path` is a specific location within Vault where secrets are stored and managed. 89 90 Here is an example of a job that uses the vault action to fetch a GitHub token 91 with read-only permission to all repos 92 93 ```yaml 94 jobs: 95 fetch-github-tokens: 96 runs-on: ubuntu-latest 97 permissions: 98 id-token: write # allows the workflow to request an OIDC token for vault auth 99 contents: read 100 steps: 101 - name: Fetch Github Tokens 102 id: fetch-github-tokens 103 uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3 104 with: 105 url: ${{ vars.VAULT_URL }} 106 method: jwt 107 path: github-jwt 108 exportEnv: false 109 role: github_uw_ro 110 secrets: | 111 /github/token/uw_ro token | GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN ; 112 113 - name: Use fetched secrets 114 run: | 115 git config --global url."https://user:${GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN}@github.com".insteadOf "https://github.com" 116 env: 117 GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN: ${{ steps.fetch-github-tokens.outputs.GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN }} 118 119 # The token generated by the vault in this job is used only within this job 120 # hence it can be revoked at the end end of the job 121 - name: Revoke token 122 if: always() 123 run: | 124 curl -sSL -H "Authorization: Bearer ${GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN}" -X DELETE https://api.github.com/installation/token 125 env: 126 GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN: ${{ steps.fetch-github-tokens.outputs.GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN }} 127 ``` 128 129 ## Deciding Which Token/Role to Use 130 131 ### Case 1: Workflow needs access to its own repository 132 133 No need to use external access token, 134 [Use GITHUB_TOKEN for authentication in workflows.](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/authenticate-with-github_token#permissions-for-the-github_token) 135 please also see [permissions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax#permissions) 136 137 ### Case 2: Workflow needs access to other repositories which is covered by a common roles 138 139 workflow can benefit from ephemeral access token. There is no need to create new permission set. 140 Use one of the available roles detailed in the [Common Roles](#common-roles). 141 142 ### Case 3: Workflow needs custom access 143 144 If your workflow needs access which is not covered by common roles, You must create a new, 145 repository-specific role and permission set. 146 please see [Repo Specific Permission Set](#repo-specific-permission-set) 147 148 149 ## Common Roles 150 Common roles are designed to be used by workflows in any `private` repository within the organization. 151 They provide a set of permissions that are generally useful across multiple repositories. 152 153 ### Available Common Roles 154 155 | Role | Secret Paths | Permissions | 156 | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | 157 | `github_uw_ro` | `/github/token/uw_ro` | read-only access to all repositories 158 | `kubernetes_manifests_releaser` | `/github/token/uw_ro`
`/github/token/kubernetes_manifests_rw` | read-only access to all repositories and
rw kubernetes-manifests repository 159 160 #### Example: Using Common Roles `kubernetes_manifests_releaser` 161 162 ```yaml 163 jobs: 164 deploy: 165 runs-on: ubuntu-latest 166 permissions: 167 id-token: write # allow OIDC token req for vault auth 168 pull-requests: write 169 contents: read 170 steps: 171 - name: Fetch Github Tokens 172 id: fetch-github-tokens 173 uses: hashicorp/vault-action@v3 174 with: 175 url: ${{ vars.VAULT_URL }} 176 method: jwt 177 path: github-jwt 178 exportEnv: false 179 role: kubernetes_manifests_releaser 180 # The secret path corresponds to the permission set name. 181 secrets: | 182 /github/token/uw_ro token | GITHUB_UW_RO_TOKEN ; 183 /github/token/kubernetes_manifests_rw token | GITHUB_K8S_TOKEN ; 184 ``` 185 186 ## Repo Specific Permission Set 187 188 If the common roles do not provide the specific permissions you need, 189 you can create a custom role that is suitable for the repository. 190 191 #### Step 1: Define a Permission Set 192 Define what permissions the token should have and to which repositories in 193 [permission_sets](https://github.com/utilitywarehouse/terraform/blob/master/github/sys-vault-repo-permission-sets/permission-sets.tf) variable. 194 195 Example: Creating a permission set for go-mono 196 197 ```hcl 198 # Perfect! Let me create a comprehensive summary of my findings. Based on my search, I have found significant documentation about Strongbox configuration, though I did not find specific documentation about "js-mono" (a potential JavaScript monorepo). Let me compile my findings:

## Summary of Strongbox Configuration Documentation

I have searched the documentation repository and found comprehensive information about Strongbox configuration, primarily focused on infrastructure and Kubernetes deployments. Here are the key findings:

### Key Documentation Files Found:

1. **Strongbox Release and Configuration Announcements:**
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/infra/announce/2020-08-19-strongbox-0.3.1-release.md` - Details about Strongbox v0.3.1 release with header format changes (key-id in header)
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/infra/announce/2024-07-29-strongbox-age.md` - Information about age encryption support for Strongbox v2, including migration from `.strongbox-keyid` to `.strongbox_recipient`

2. **Kubernetes Integration Documentation:**
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/infra/kubernetes/using-kube-applier.md` - Complete guide for Strongbox integration with kube-applier, including:
     - Creating Kubernetes Secrets with strongbox keyrings
     - Waybill configuration with `strongboxKeyringSecretRef`
     - Examples for both Kustomize and plain manifest configurations
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/infra/kubernetes/argoCD.md` - ArgoCD integration with Strongbox via the argocd-voodoobox-plugin, including namespace-specific keyring secrets

3. **Infrastructure as Code (Terraform):**
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/tech_stack/rds/postgres.md` - RDS setup guide showing how to:
     - Add team strongbox keys via `.strongbox-keyid` file
     - Encrypt Terraform variables files (terraform.tfvars) with strongbox
     - Configure `.gitattributes` for encrypted files

4. **Monorepo and Docker Information:**
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/tech_stack/docker/learnings.md` - Mentions TypeScript backend monorepos but notes that the Go monorepo approach couldn't be directly copied for TypeScript/JavaScript repos. No "js-mono" specific configuration found.

5. **Worklog References:**
   - `/Users/dumitruvulpe/uw/documentation/infra/worklog/worklog-2021.md` - Historical context on file ordering issues with `.strongbox-keyid` and key-id header implementation
   - Various worklog entries mentioning strongbox setup for different repositories (customer-support-mono, partner-mono, caps-mono, data-infra-mono, go-mono)

### Key Strongbox Configuration Patterns:

- **Traditional Key ID**: `.strongbox-keyid` (older format)
- **Age Recipients**: `.strongbox_recipient` (newer format with age encryption)
- **Keyring Secrets in Kubernetes**: `kube-applier-strongbox-keyring` and `argocd-voodoobox-strongbox-keyring` secrets
- **Encryption Files**: `.gitattributes` used to mark files for encryption

### Note on JavaScript/Node.js Monorepos:

The documentation references several monorepos (go-mono, partner-mono, caps-mono, etc.) but does **not contain specific documentation about "js-mono" or JavaScript monorepo Strongbox configuration**. The Docker learnings document mentions that TypeScript backend monorepos exist but cannot simply reuse Go monorepo CI/CD approaches, but no specific configuration guide for js-mono was found.
